published.text:
### Hero
Badge: Comparison Guide
Heading: n8n vs Zapier for Small Business
Body: Both tools automate workflows. But they solve different problems at different price points. This page helps you figure out which one fits your business.
Sub: No affiliate links. No vendor sponsorship. Just a practical breakdown based on building with both tools for real businesses.
CTA: Get Help Choosing | All Guides

### The short version
Intro: If you need a quick decision framework, here it is:
Zapier is probably enough if:
- You need simple triggers: when X happens, do Y
- You use mainstream SaaS tools (Gmail, Slack, HubSpot, Sheets)
- You want zero setup and zero maintenance
- Your automations are under 5 steps each
- You are not technical and do not have a developer on call
n8n is the better fit if:
- Your workflows have branching logic, loops, or error handling
- You need to connect to APIs that Zapier does not support natively
- You want to own your data and not depend on a third-party cloud
- Your Zapier bill is climbing past $100/month
- You have a developer or consultant who can build and maintain it

### What each tool actually is
Zapier: Cloud-only. No-code. Connects 6,000+ apps with a trigger-action model. Optimized for speed-to-value.
n8n: Open-source. Visual workflow builder with a code option. Can be self-hosted or used via n8n Cloud. Optimized for flexibility.

### Side-by-side comparison
Table comparing Zapier vs n8n across: Pricing, Ease of use, Complexity ceiling, App integrations, Data ownership, Maintenance, Best for.

### The cost question
Three cards:
- Zapier costs scale with volume: Per-task pricing. A 5-step workflow processing 1,000 records/month uses 5,000 tasks. Monthly bills can reach $200 to $800+ at higher volumes.
- n8n costs scale with complexity: Cloud charges by workflow count, not task volume. Self-hosted costs $5 to $50/month for server. Setup and maintenance take more effort.
- The hidden cost is time: Zapier saves time upfront but costs more at scale. n8n saves money at scale but costs more time upfront. Break-even is around $100 to $150/month in Zapier spend.

### When this decision actually matters
Four scenarios: High-volume lead processing, Complex conditional logic, Custom API connections, Data privacy requirements.
Closing: If none of these apply and your automations are straightforward, Zapier is a fine choice. Do not over-engineer the decision.

### Who should use which
Stick with Zapier: Non-technical, simple workflows, popular SaaS tools, manageable task volume, no data residency needs.
Move to n8n: Zapier bill over $100/month, need conditions/loops/error handling, custom APIs, want to own infrastructure, have dev/consultant available.

### See how n8n works in practice
Two case study cards linking to published pages (Instagram lead gen, E-commerce CRM).
Links to: /case-studies, /ai-automation-consultant-small-business

### FAQ — Common questions
4 Q&As covering: migration from Zapier to n8n, n8n reliability, whether you need a developer, Make as an alternative.

### Final CTA
Heading: Not sure which tool fits your business?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will look at your workflows, your current tools, and figure out the right automation platform and approach together.
Sub: No sales pitch. Just a clear recommendation based on your specific situation.
Benefits: 30-minute focused call / Platform recommendation for your use case / Leave with a clear next step
Embedded booking widget.

edits.description: Comparison/decision page, not a review or blog post. Structured to help the reader choose, not just learn.
Hero uses a plain statement headline, not a question or clever copy.
"The short version" section gives the answer upfront for scanners, then the rest of the page supports the reasoning.
Tone is practical and neutral. Does not favor either tool. Acknowledges Zapier is fine for simple use cases.
No em dashes in published copy. Uses periods, commas, or colons instead.
Cost section uses concrete numbers and ranges, not vague claims.
"When this decision actually matters" section prevents over-engineering by noting when the choice does not matter much.
Case study references link to real published pages.
FAQ answers are specific and honest. Acknowledges Make as a middle option.
CTA is framed as "get help choosing" rather than selling a specific tool.
Uses the site's standard Section wrapper, card patterns, table layout, and Framer Motion animations.
Booking widget is self-contained on this page.
